heading: MODULUS DECIMUSSEPTIMUS
card_summary: Modulus Decimusseptimus opens the seventeenth module of the Eighth Tone with the single-word rubric "Unio" and the heading "De beatorum unione cum Deo" — on the union of the blessed with God. Zorzi anchors the discussion in Christ's high-priestly prayer from the Gospel of John (chapters 17), citing the words "Pater sancte serva eos ... ut sint unum, sicut et nos" and the intercessory passage for all future believers "ut omnes unum sint, sicut tu Pater in me, et ego in te." He then turns to Plotinus as a philosophical witness, quoting the Neoplatonist on the secrecy surrounding mystical initiation and the prohibition against disclosing divine things to the uninitiated. The module invokes Parmenides's doctrine that "all things are one," defended against Aristotelian criticism through the voice of the commentator Simplicius, who argues that Aristotle argued against the words, not the meaning, of Parmenides. The processio-and-return schema underlies the whole: all things proceed from the One and must strip away multiplicity — imagination, discursive reason, sense — to return to it.
essay:
# MODULUS DECIMUSSEPTIMUS — "De beatorum unione cum Deo"

## Title and Translation

*Modulus Decimusseptimus* is the seventeenth modulus (module or measure) within the Eighth Tone (*Tonus Octavus*) of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (Venice 1525; Italian edition ed. Saverio Campanini, Bompiani 2010). The single-word rubric that immediately follows the title is **Unio** — Union — and the full descriptive heading reads *De beatorum unione cum Deo, Concentus Unicus*: "On the union of the blessed with God, a single harmony." The musical metaphors of *tonus*, *modulus*, and *concentus* are characteristic of Zorzi's organising conceit throughout the work: sacred doctrine is arranged as a cosmic musical score.

The term *modulus* derives from the Latin *modus* (measure, manner, mode), carrying resonances of both mathematical proportion and musical interval. In the context of Renaissance harmonic theology, the modulus functions as a discrete harmonic unit within a larger tonal structure—each module represents a complete harmonic gesture or doctrinal statement that contributes to the total consonance of the work. The Eighth Tone itself is the ultimate tone in Zorzi's elaborate system, reserved for the mysteries that exceed ordinary rational apprehension. By designating this module as the seventeenth and placing it in the eighth tone, Zorzi signals its eschatological significance: it addresses not the present order of creation but the final reversion (*reductio*) of all being to its divine source.

The choice of musical language is philosophically deliberate. In medieval and Renaissance aesthetics, music stands at the intersection of mathematics, physics, and theology. A perfect interval represents not merely a pleasing sound but a ratio expressing metaphysical truth; harmonious conjunction embodies the divine order impressed upon creation. When Zorzi invokes *concentus* (a joining-together of distinct tones into unified sound), he gestures toward the paradox that will dominate this module: how multiplicity returns to unity without the destruction of either pole. The blessed do not dissolve into undifferentiated absorption; rather, they achieve a union that preserves distinction within unity, much as the octave preserves the individual notes even as it encompasses them in a higher register.

## What the Section Introduces

This title page signals that the seventeenth module will be devoted entirely to the doctrine of mystical union — the eschatological and contemplative merging of the sanctified soul with the divine. The rubric *Concentus Unicus* ("a single harmony") implies that the union to be discussed is not merely moral or intellectual but ontological: a collapse of multiplicity back into the primordial One.

In theological tradition, the doctrine of mystical union occupies contested ground. Christian scholasticism had generally distinguished the soul's union with God from the hypostatic union of Christ—the beatific vision grants unprecedented proximity and knowledge but stops short of abolishing the creature-Creator distinction. Neoplatonic mysticism, by contrast, spoke of henosis, a literal merger of consciousness into the divine monad, at least at the apex of the contemplative ascent. Zorzi's task in this module is to navigate between these positions: to honor the Christian insistence on God's transcendence and the soul's creaturely status while simultaneously embracing the Neoplatonic language of mystical ascent and union. This tension will structure the entire argument.

The placement of this module at the culmination of the Eighth Tone suggests a climactic vision. The earlier tones have traced the procession of being from God through the celestial hierarchies, the intellectual realm, the sensible cosmos, and the fallen human condition. Now, in the seventeenth module of the final tone, the trajectory reverses. The blessed—those who have been sanctified through Christ and have ascended the contemplative ladder—are shown returning to God through a union so radical that it exceeds the categories of ordinary experience. This module thus represents the telos, the end-goal, toward which the entire harmonic structure of *De Harmonia Mundi* is oriented. It is the resolution in which all the discordant frequencies of created being find their ultimate consonance.

## Opening Movement

Zorzi begins immediately with the scriptural warrant: Christ's intercessory prayer in John 17, the petition "that they may be one, as we are one" (*ut sint unum, sicut et nos*). The verse is read not as a metaphor for ecclesial solidarity but as a disclosure of the very *modus* — the manner — of divine union. Christ's words "as you, Father, are in me and I in you" supply the ontological template.

The selection of John 17 is theologically strategic. This chapter, often called the "high priestly prayer" or "prayer of consecration," represents Christ's final discourse to the disciples before his passion. It is eschatological in orientation, already anticipating the post-resurrection state in which disciples will be united to Christ and through him to the Father. Zorzi quotes the double petition: first, that the disciples themselves "may be one, as we are one" (*ut sint unum, sicut et nos*); second, the intercession for future believers—"I do not pray for these only, but also for those who believe in me through their word, that they may all be one; even as thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee, that they also may be in us" (*ut omnes unum sint, sicut tu Pater in me, et ego in te*).

The theological weight here cannot be overstated. Christ does not merely petition for moral agreement or spiritual fellowship. He presents the Trinity as the paradigm: the Father and Son are one, not through external union but through mutual indwelling (*perichoresis*). The disciples' union with one another and with the Godhead is patterned on this trinitarian model. Zorzi will use this Johannine framework throughout the module to ground the mystical theology in the very structure of divine revelation. The union of the blessed with God is not an invention of mysticism but an explicit topic of Christ's prayer, embedded in the canonical scripture at its most solemn and definitive moment.

Moreover, the Johannine passage introduces a subtle but crucial distinction. Christ prays not only for the immediate disciples but "for those who will believe in me through their word." This extends union across time and space—it is not a localized mystical event but a transhistorical reality that encompasses all the redeemed in every age. The union of the blessed is thus simultaneously individual (each soul in its unique relation to God) and corporate (all the blessed as a single mystical body). The *Concentus Unicus* ("single harmony") terminology captures this paradox: many voices, one consonance.

## Plotinus as Philosophical Witness

Zorzi then summons Plotinus as the supreme pagan witness to this same mystery, quoting him on the duty of silence surrounding initiation into the highest realities. The invocation of Parmenides — defended by Simplicius against Aristotle's merely verbal objections — establishes the Neoplatonic metaphysical frame: all things proceed from the One and are drawn by necessity to return, shedding the veils of imagination, discursive reason, and sense that Aristotle never transcended.

The appeal to Plotinus (204-270 CE) represents one of the most daring moves in Renaissance Christian Neoplatonism. Plotinus himself was pagan; his mysticism was untethered to revelation and developed through purely philosophical investigation. Yet Zorzi reads in Plotinus a profound wisdom about the highest reaches of contemplation—a wisdom that does not contradict Christian doctrine but rather illuminates dimensions that discursive theology struggles to articulate. This move was not original to Zorzi; it had precedent in the Pseudo-Dionysius (sixth century), who had synthesized Neoplatonic metaphysics with Christian apophatic theology, and in the medieval mystical tradition (Meister Eckhart, the Cloud of Unknowing). But Zorzi's engagement with Plotinus is unusually direct and sustained.

The specific point Zorzi emphasizes is Plotinus's injunction concerning secrecy. The Enneads are filled with passages warning that the highest mysteries of henosis cannot be disclosed to the uninitiated—not because they are obscure but because they transcend conceptual formulation. What cannot be spoken of in ordinary language cannot be conveyed through written exposition alone. This silence is not evasion but reverence; it acknowledges that the highest reaches of mystical experience exceed the capacity of discursive reason. Zorzi adopts this hermeneutic stance: the union of the blessed with God is knowable through contemplative ascent but not fully expressible in doctrinal propositions. Language can point toward it; only direct experience can verify it.

In Plotinus's metaphysical system, the One is utterly transcendent—beyond being, beyond intellect, beyond all determination. From the One emanates Intellect (*Nous*), which contains the eternal archetypes of all things; from Intellect emanates Soul, which mediates between the intelligible and sensible realms. The human soul, being divine in origin, can ascend the ladder of being by purifying itself of sensory attachment, training discursive reason (*dianoia*) to yield to intellectual intuition (*noesis*), and ultimately achieving henosis—a temporary union or mystical touching of the One itself. This ascent is not earned through moral effort alone but demands a simultaneous grace-like descent of the One toward the soul. The movement is mutual, reciprocal, henotic. Zorzi's use of Plotinus establishes that such a mystical ascent is not theologically suspect but represents the apex of human spiritual possibility.

Furthermore, Plotinus's insistence that the union cannot be sustained indefinitely—that the soul "falls back" into Intellect after the henotic moment—will prove crucial to Zorzi's careful distinction between the eschatological union of the blessed (permanent, after bodily resurrection) and the provisional mystical touches granted to contemplatives in the present life. The contrast illustrates that while Plotinian philosophy provides a phenomenology of mystical experience, Christian doctrine supplies the eschatological fulfillment that Platonism could not guarantee.

## Parmenides and the Defense of Non-Duality

The invocation of Parmenides introduces the ancient root of Neoplatonic monism. Parmenides's fragmentary poem (fifth century BCE) asserts the radical doctrine: "What is, is; what is not, is not; being is one, eternal, immutable, and homogeneous." The apparent multiplicity of the world is illusion; true reality is singular and undifferentiated. This doctrine had been the target of Aristotle's sustained critique (particularly in the *Metaphysics*), where Aristotle argues that Parmenides and other monists had confused linguistic and logical categories with ontological reality—they were arguing against words, not against what actually exists.

Zorzi cites Simplicius, the Neoplatonic commentator (sixth century CE), in defense of Parmenides against this Aristotelian objection. Simplicius argues that Aristotle was indeed attacking the letter of Parmenides while missing the meaning. The deeper truth that Parmenides sought to express—that all multiplicity proceeds from and is sustained by a single source—remains valid even if crude monism (denying any real distinction whatsoever) is untenable. For Simplicius and the Neoplatonic tradition, multiplicity and unity are not contradictory but complementary: the many genuinely proceed from the One yet remain eternally dependent upon it. Real distinction exists at the level of manifestation, but ultimate reality is unified.

This philosophical move is essential to Zorzi's entire project. He must explain how the blessed can be "one" with God without absurdist consequences (absorption into divine substance, loss of individual consciousness, indistinguishability from the Creator). The Neoplatonic scheme, properly understood, permits this: the One remains utterly unified and transcendent; the many proceed from it in a hierarchy of progressively differentiated beings; yet the many never truly depart from the One—they are always contained within it. The blessed soul in union with God does not cease to be itself; rather, it knows itself and God simultaneously in an intuition that transcends the subject-object distinction of ordinary consciousness.

The defense of Parmenides also establishes a hermeneutical principle crucial to Zorzi's method: ancient pagan philosophers, rightly read, contain profound truths that anticipate Christian revelation. This is not syncretism in the pejorative sense but the conviction that divine wisdom (*sophia*) has been dispersed throughout human history and cultural traditions, and the Christian revelation gathers and perfects what was scattered. To read Parmenides with Simplicius is to recover a pre-Christian witness to the metaphysical structure that Christianity will fulfill and transcend. The doctrine of union with God, then, is not foreign to human spiritual aspiration; it represents the perennial goal toward which all genuine mysticism, whether Greek, Jewish, or Christian, aims.

## The Processio-and-Return Schema

The processio-and-return schema underlies the whole: all things proceed from the One and must strip away multiplicity — imagination, discursive reason, sense — to return to it. This schema, fundamentally Neoplatonic in origin, becomes the organizing principle of Zorzi's entire cosmological vision.

The doctrine of *processio* (emanation) and *reductio* (return) represents one of the most sophisticated metaphysical structures developed in antiquity and the medieval period. It avoids both the trap of strict dualism (matter is evil, spirit is good, redemption requires escape from the material realm) and the trap of pantheism (God and creation are ultimately identical). Instead, it posits a continuous gradient: all beings emanate from the One in concentric waves or hierarchical levels; each lower level bears the imprint of the level above it; and each being, by following its proper nature, spontaneously tends back toward its source.

In human terms, this means the soul's descent into matter (necessary for individual embodied existence) is matched by a potential ascent back to the divine. The process of return is not unnatural or violent but the realization of the soul's truest nature. The soul begins in union with the divine, descends through the intelligible and sensible realms, becomes entangled in bodily sensation and passion, and can—through ascetical discipline and contemplative practice—retrace its steps back to its source. The final union with God is thus, paradoxically, a homecoming—a recovery of what was always the soul's deepest identity.

Zorzi's specification of what must be "stripped away" is particularly instructive. He lists imagination, discursive reason, and sense. In the Neoplatonic hierarchy of cognitive faculties, sense perception is the lowest—it grasps only the flux of sensible appearance. Discursive reason (*dianoia*) is higher—it operates through sequential logic, analysis, and conceptual thought. Imagination (*phantasia*) is intermediate—it forms mental images and combines sensory data into larger wholes. Above all these stands Intellect (*noesis*)—immediate, non-sequential intuition of intelligible forms in their eternal presence. And beyond Intellect lies henosis—the simple touching of the One beyond all intellection.

The return to God thus requires a systematic stripping away of cruder cognitive modes in favor of progressively more subtle ones, culminating in a form of knowing that exceeds knowing itself. This is not anti-intellectual; rather, it sets reason in its proper place. Reason is invaluable for purifying the soul of sensory attachment and erroneous opinion, but it must ultimately yield to a higher faculty. The contemplative ascent is therefore educational—it trains the soul through multiple stages—but it also involves a radical kenosis, an emptying, where the ego-driven will finally falls silent and lets the One act within consciousness.

The eschatological implication is profound: the blessed, having completed this return in fullness, will abide in permanent henotic union. Unlike the mystical touches granted to contemplatives in the present life (which are transient, difficult to sustain, and followed by a descent), the eschatological union is stable and eternal. The soul does not merely touch the divine but is thoroughly integrated into its light. This is the ultimate *consonantia*—not a discord resolved but a perfect harmony where distinction and unity coexist without tension.

## Theological and Cosmological Implications

The cosmological implications of this doctrine are staggering. If all things proceed from the One and return to it, then the entire cosmos is fundamentally unified, despite its appearance of multiplicity. History itself becomes teleological—not a meaningless flux but a movement toward an end (telos) in which all beings find their completion. This completion is not destructive but perfective: the cosmos does not cease to exist but is transformed into its fullest, most harmonious state.

For Zorzi, this cosmic vision is explicitly Christological. Christ is the pivot-point where the descending and ascending movements meet. In the Incarnation, God's self-outpouring reaches its lowest extremity—the Word becomes flesh, descending into matter, suffering, and death. In the Resurrection and Ascension, Christ reverses the descent, drawing all creation back to God. The Church, as Christ's mystical body, participates in this double movement: it exists in the tension between the already-fulfilled redemption (Christ's victory) and the not-yet-complete eschatological consummation. The union of the blessed with God is thus the fulfillment of Christ's work, the final gathering-in (*recapitulatio*) of all things.

The harmonic theology that structures *De Harmonia Mundi* is built on this vision. Each being, from the highest angel to the lowest creature, has its proper place and function in the cosmic symphony. Disharmony arises when beings revolt against their proper nature—when the soul cleaves to sensory indulgence instead of ascending toward God, when pride and ambition corrupt the proper order. Harmony is restored through redemption—through the soul's re-orientation toward its divine source. The ultimate *concentus unicus* is the eschaton, when all the redeemed, having been perfected and transfigured, sing in perfect unison the praises of the One.

## Connection to Broader De Harmonia Mundi Themes

This module's doctrine of union must be read against the backdrop of the entire work's argument. *De Harmonia Mundi* is fundamentally about proportion, ratio, and correspondence. The universe is organized according to mathematical principles—the same ratios that produce consonant intervals in music also govern the distances of the planets, the structure of the human body, the hierarchies of being. This is not mere poetic fancy but a deep conviction about the intelligibility of creation. God, the supreme intelligence and geometer, has stamped the signature of divine wisdom upon every level of being.

The doctrine of union with God represents the culmination of this harmonic vision. Union is the final and perfect proportion—the ratio in which the creature is perfectly attuned to the Creator, where the soul's frequencies synchronize with God's infinite measure. In mystical theology, this was sometimes expressed through the metaphor of the string: the human soul is like a lute string tuned to the divine pitch; when properly attuned, it vibrates in sympathetic resonance with the celestial music. This is not fanciful; it expresses a deep truth about the mystical union—it is a kind of attunement, a bringing-into-harmony of what was discordant.

The harmonic theme also illuminates why Zorzi insists so strongly on the Neoplatonic language of the One and the Many. Traditional scholastic theology had developed increasingly abstract language for God: He is being itself, subsistent existence, the ground of being. These formulations, while logically precise, can feel distant from the lived experience of mystical prayer. The Neoplatonic One, by contrast, is intimate—the source from which all beings flow, the inexhaustible fountain that remains full while giving forth, the silence from which all meaning emerges. By identifying God with the Neoplatonic One, Zorzi aims to recover a more experiential, mystical dimension of theology without sacrificing doctrinal precision.

Furthermore, the processio-reductio schema explains why the cosmos is not merely useful but good and beautiful. If creation were merely a punishment or a testing-ground, matter would be inherently evil, and escape would be the only salvation. But if creation is the effusion of divine goodness, if all things flow from God in structured hierarchy and return to Him in a great cycle of manifestation and reversion, then the cosmos is charged with theological significance. Every creature is a step on the ladder; every created beauty reflects (however dimly) the infinite beauty of the source. The mystical vision here is therefore deeply incarnational: the physical world is not a prison to escape but a school in which to learn, and ultimately to behold with transformed eyes.

The unity achieved in mystical union is thus not a denial of creation but its ultimate affirmation. The blessed see with God's eyes and recognize every creature in its truth—comprehending, as God comprehends, how each being participates in divine goodness. The final harmony is achieved when human vision is lifted to the divine perspective, where multiplicity and unity, individuation and universality, particular beauty and infinite perfection are seen to be eternally reconciled in the mind of God.
